context_1.text:
Two serious canal routes are on the table. The Panama route offers a shorter crossing — 50 miles — and benefits from partial French excavation begun by Ferdinand de Lesseps in the 1880s before the project collapsed in scandal. The New Panama Canal Company, desperate to recover its investment, is willing to sell its assets for $40 million.

The Nicaragua route is longer — 170 miles — but legally simpler. Nicaragua has no prior canal agreements and is eager to negotiate. Crucially, it runs through a volcanic region: Mount Momotombo erupted in 1902, and Congressman William Hepburn reportedly circulated a Nicaraguan postage stamp featuring Mount Momotombo in eruption — using the image to warn colleagues off the volcanic route. Whether the stamp truly swayed votes is disputed by historians, but it became part of the canal debate's lore.

The Isthmian Canal Commission has shifted its recommendation from Nicaragua to Panama — but Congress and the public remain divided.

outcome.text:
The Panama Canal is under construction. The United States has done in two years what the French could not accomplish in a decade.

In 1911, speaking at the University of California, Theodore Roosevelt would put it with characteristic bluntness: "I took the isthmus and let Congress debate." He was describing exactly what happened — and exactly why Colombia, and Latin America at large, would not forget it.

The canal you helped build will open in 1914. It will be one of the great engineering achievements of the 20th century. The way it was acquired — through a revolution the U.S. enabled, against a nation whose sovereignty was overridden — will define how the United States is seen in the hemisphere for generations.

Your advisors will carry their judgments into the next chapter. The precedents set in Panama echo in every lesson that follows.

decision_2.default: Colombia's senate had every right to reject the Hay-Herrán Treaty — the original terms were frankly insulting. We offered $10 million once and $250,000 annually. A more generous proposal might succeed.
decision_2.trusted: I want you to see something. In 1909, Secretary Knox wrote that the U.S. was convinced a revolution in Nicaragua represented "the ideals and the will of a majority." He wrote that to justify backing a revolt that served American interests. Now we're doing the same thing in Panama. What Colombia thinks of the Hay-Herrán Treaty matters less, apparently, than what we want built through it.
